title: Necessary and sufficient graphical conditions for optimal adjustment sets in causal graphical models with hidden variables [PDF]

paragraphs.text: ## **Necessary and sufficient graphical conditions for optimal adjustment sets in causal graphical models with hidden variables**

## **Jakob Runge**

German Aerospace Center Institute of Data Science 07745 Jena, Germany and Technische Universität Berlin 10623 Berlin, Germany `jakob.runge@dlr.de`

## **Abstract**

The problem of selecting optimal backdoor adjustment sets to estimate causal effects in graphical models with hidden and conditioned variables is addressed. Previous work has defined optimality as achieving the smallest asymptotic estimation variance and derived an optimal set for the case without hidden variables. For the case with hidden variables there can be settings where no optimal set exists and currently only a sufficient graphical optimality criterion of limited applicability has been derived. In the present work optimality is characterized as maximizing a certain adjustment information which allows to derive a necessary and sufficient graphical criterion for the existence of an optimal adjustment set and a definition and algorithm to construct it. Further, the optimal set is valid if and only if a valid adjustment set exists and has higher (or equal) adjustment information than the Adjust-set proposed in Perkovi´c et al. [Journal of Machine Learning Research, 18: 1–62, 2018] for any graph. The results translate to minimal asymptotic estimation variance for a class of estimators whose asymptotic variance follows a certain information-theoretic relation. Numerical experiments indicate that the asymptotic results also hold for relatively small sample sizes and that the optimal adjustment set or minimized variants thereof often yield better variance also beyond that estimator class. Surprisingly, among the randomly created setups more than 90% fulfill the optimality conditions indicating that also in many real-world scenarios graphical optimality may hold.

## **1 Introduction**

A standard problem setting in causal inference is to estimate the causal effect between two variables given a causal graphical model that specifies qualitative causal relations among observed variables [Pearl, 2009], including a possible presence of hidden confounding variables. The graphical model then allows to employ graphical criteria to identify valid adjustment sets, the most well-known being the _backdoor criterion_ [Pearl, 1993] and the _generalized adjustment criterion_ [Shpitser et al., 2010, Perkovi´c et al., 2015, 2018], providing a complete identification of all valid adjustment sets. Estimators of causal effects based on such a valid adjustment set as a covariate are then unbiased, but for different adjustment sets the estimation variance may strongly vary. An _optimal adjustment set_ may be characterized as one that has minimal asymptotic estimation variance. In **current work** , following Kuroki and Cai [2004] and Kuroki and Miyakawa [2003], Henckel et al. [2019] (abbreviated

35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021).

HPM19 in the following) showed that graphical optimality always holds for linear models in the causally sufficient case where all relevant variables are observed. In Witte et al. [2020] an alternative characterization of the optimal adjustment set is discussed and the approach was integrated into the IDA algorithm [Maathuis et al., 2009, 2010] that does not require the causal graph to be known. Rotnitzky and Smucler [2019] extended the results in HPM19 to asymptotically linear non-parametric graphical models. HPM19’s optimal adjustment set holds for the causally sufficient case (no hidden variables) and the authors gave an example with hidden variables where optimality does not hold in general, i.e., the optimal adjustment set depends on the coefficients and noise terms (more generally, the distribution), rather than just the graph. Most recently, Smucler et al. [2021] (SSR20) partially extended these results to the non-parametric hidden variables case together with _dynamic treatment regimes_ , i.e., conditional causal effects. SSR20 provide a sufficient criterion for an optimal set to exist and a definition based on a certain undirected graph-construction using a result by van der Zander et al. [2019]. However, their sufficient criterion is very restrictive and a current major open problem is a _necessary_ and sufficient condition for an optimal adjustment set to exist in the hidden variable case and a corresponding definition of an optimal set.

My **main theoretical contribution** is a solution to this problem. Optimality for conditional causal effects in the hidden variables case is fully characterized by an information-theoretic approach involving a certain difference of conditional mutual informations among the observed variables termed the adjustment information. Maximizing the adjustment information formalizes the common intuition to choose adjustment sets that maximally constrain the effect variable and minimally constrain the cause variable. This allows to derive a necessary and sufficient graphical criterion for the existence of an optimal adjustment set. The derived optimal adjustment set also has the property of minimum cardinality, i.e., no node can be removed without sacrificing optimality. Further, the optimal set is valid if and only if a valid adjustment set exists and has higher (or equal) adjustment information than the Adjust-set proposed in Perkovi´c et al. [2018] for any graph, whether graphical optimality holds or not. The results translate to minimal asymptotic estimation variance for a class of estimators whose asymptotic variance follows a certain information-theoretic relation that, at present, I could only verify theoretically for the linear case. As **practical contributions** the paper provides extensive numerical experiments that corroborate the theoretical results and show that the optimal adjustment set or minimized variants thereof often yield better variance also beyond the theoretically analyzed estimator class. Code is available in the python package `https://github.com/jakobrunge/tigramite` . More detailed preliminaries, proofs, algorithms, and further numerical experiments are given in the Supplementary Material.

## **1.1 Preliminaries and problem setting**

We consider causal effects in causal graphical models over a set of variables **V** with a joint distribution _P_ = _P_ ( **V** ) that is consistent with an acyclic directed mixed graph (ADMG) _G_ = ( **V** _, E_ ). Two nodes can have possibly more than one edge which can be _directed_ ( _←_ ) or _bi-directed_ ( _↔_ ). See Fig. 1A for an example. Kinships are defined as usual: parents _pa_ ( _X_ ) for “ _•→X_ ”, spouses _sp_ ( _X_ ) for “ _X↔•_ ”, children _ch_ ( _X_ ) for “ _X→•_ ”. These sets all exclude _X_ . Correspondingly descendants _des_ ( _X_ ) and ancestors _an_ ( _X_ ) are defined, which, on the other hand, both include _X_ . The mediator nodes on causal paths from _X_ to _Y_ are denoted **M** = **M** ( _X, Y_ ) and exclude _X_ and _Y_ . For detailed preliminaries, including the definition of open and blocked paths, see Supplementary Section A. In this work we only consider a univariate intervention variable _X_ and effect variable _Y_ . We simplify set notation and denote unions of variables as _{W } ∪_ **M** _∪_ **A** = _W_ **MA** .

A (possibly empty) set of adjustment variables **Z** for the total causal effect of _X_ on _Y_ in an ADMG is called _valid_ relative to ( _X, Y_ ) if the interventional distribution for setting _do_ ( _X_ = **x** ) [Pearl, 2009] factorizes as _p_ ( _Y |do_ ( _X_ = **x** )) = � _p_ ( _Y |_ **x** _,_ **z** ) _p_ ( **z** ) _d_ **z** for non-empty **Z** and as _p_ ( _Y |do_ ( _X_ = **x** )) = _p_ ( _Y |_ **x** ) for empty **Z** . Valid adjustment sets, the set of which is here denoted _Z_ , can be read off from a given causal graph using the generalized adjustment criterion [Perkovi´c et al., 2015, 2018] which generalizes Pearl’s back-door criterion [Pearl, 2009]. A set **Z** is valid if both of the following conditions hold: (i) **Z** _∩_ **forb** = _∅_ , and (ii) all non-causal paths from _X_ to _Y_ are blocked by **Z** . An adjustment set is called _minimal_ if no strict subset of **Z** is still valid. We assume a non-zero causal effect from X on Y , potentially through a set of mediators_ **M** _, and given selected conditioned variables_ **S** _, where_ **S** _∩_ **forb** = _∅. ## **2 Optimal adjustment sets**

## **2.1 Information-theoretic characterization**

Figure 1B illustrates two causal effect estimates for a linear Gaussian model consistent with the graph in Fig. 1A. With **Z** = _Z_ 1 _Z_ 2 (blue) the error is much larger than with **O** = _Z_ 2 _Z_ 3 (orange) for two reasons: **Z** constrains the residual variance _V ar_ ( _Y |_ **ZS** ) of the effect variable _Y_ less than **O** and, on the other hand, **Z** constrains the residual variance _V ar_ ( _X|_ **ZS** ) of the cause variable _X_ more than **O** . Smaller estimator variance also holds for **O** compared to any other valid set in _Z_ here.

We information-theoretically formalize the resulting intuition to choose an adjustment set **Z** that maximally constrains the effect variable _Y_ and minimally constrains the cause variable _X_ . In terms of CMIs and given selected fixed conditions **S** the quantity to maximize can be stated as follows.

**Definition 1** (Adjustment information) **.** _Consider a causal effect of X on Y for an adjustment set_ **Z** _given a condition set_ **S** _. The_ (conditional) adjustment (set) information _, abbreviated J_ **Z** _, is defined as_

_J_ **Z** is not necessarily positive if the dependence between _X_ and **Z** (given **S** ) is larger than that between **Z** and _Y_ given _X_ **S** . Equation (6) follows from the CMI definition. Fig. 1C illustrates the two CMIs in Eq. (5) in a Venn diagram.

Before discussing the range of estimators for which maximizing the adjustment information _J_ **Z** leads to a minimal asymptotic estimation variance in Sect. 2.2, we characterize graphical optimality in an information-theoretic framework. Our goal is to provide graphical criteria for optimal adjustment sets, i.e., criteria that depend only on the structure of the graph _G_ and not on the distribution.

**Definition 2** (Information-theoretical graphical optimality) **.** _Given Assumptions 1 we say that_ (information-theoretical) graphical optimality holds _if there is a_ **Z** _∈Z such that either there is no other_ **Z** _[′]_ = **Z** _∈Z or for all other_ **Z** _[′]_ = **Z** _∈Z and_ all _distributions P consistent with G we have J_ **Z** _≥ J_ **Z** _′._

My main result builds on the following lemma which relates graphical optimality to informationtheoretic inequalities in a necessary and sufficient comparison condition for an optimal set to exist. **Lemma 1** (Necessary and sufficient comparison criterion for existence of an optimal set) **.** _Given Assumptions 1, if and only if there is a_ **Z** _∈Z such that either there is no other_ **Z** _[′]_ = **Z** _∈Z or for all other_ **Z** _[′]_ = **Z** _∈Z and all distributions P consistent with G it holds that_

_then graphical optimality holds and_ **Z** _is optimal implying J_ **Z** _≥ J_ **Z** _′._

In SSR20 and HPM19 the corresponding conditional independence statements to the terms (iii) and (iv) in the inequalities (7) are used as a sufficient pairwise comparison criterion. However, Lemma 1 shows that for graphical optimality it is not necessary that terms (iii) and (iv) vanish, they just need to fulfill the inequalities (7) for a _necessary_ and sufficient criterion.

In principle, Lemma 1 can be used to cross-compare all pairs of sets, but firstly, it is difficult to explicitly evaluate (7) for all distributions _P_ consistent with _G_ and, secondly, iterating through all valid adjustment sets is computationally prohibitive even for small graph sizes. As an example, consider a confounding path consisting of 5 nodes. Then this path can be blocked by 2[5] _−_ 1 different subsets. In the main result of this work (Thm. 3) a necessary and sufficient criterion based purely on graphical properties is given.

## **2.3 Definition of O-set**

The optimal adjustment set for the causally sufficient case is simply **P** = _pa_ ( _Y_ **M** ) _\_ **forb** and was derived in HPM19 and Rotnitzky and Smucler [2019]. In Section B.2 the derivation is discussed from an information-theoretic perspective. In the case with hidden variables we need to account for bidirected edges “ _↔_ ” which considerably complicate the situation. Then the parents of _Y_ **M** are not sufficient to block all non-causal paths. Further, just like conditioning on parents of _Y_ **M** leads to optimality in the sufficient case since parents constrain information in _Y_ **M** , in the hidden variables case also conditioning on spouses of _Y_ **M** constrains information about _Y_ **M** .

**Example A.** A simple graph (ADMG) to illustrate this is _X→Y ↔Z_ 1 (shown with an additional **S** in Fig. 2A below, or Fig. 4 in SSR20). Here **Z** _[′]_ = _∅_ = **vancs** is a valid set, but it is not optimal. Consider **O** = _Z_ 1, then term (iii) = 0 in the inequalities (7) since **Z** _[′] \_ **O** = _∅_ . Even though not needed to block non-causal paths (there is none), _Z_ 1 still constrains information in _Y_ while being independent of _X_ (hence, term (iv) = 0) which leads to _J_ **O** _> J∅_ according to the inequalities (7).

Not only direct spouses can constrain information in _Y_ as Fig. 2B below illustrates. Since for _W ∈ Y_ **M** the motif “ _W ↔ C_ 1 _←∗C_ 2” (“ _∗_ ” denotes either edge mark) is open, it holds that _I_ ( _C_ 1 _C_ 2; _W_ ) = _I_ ( _C_ 1; _Y_ )+ _I_ ( _C_ 2; _W |C_ 1) _≥ I_ ( _C_ 1; _Y_ ) and we can even further increase the first term in the adjustment

In the following we will abbreviate **O** = **O** ( _X, Y,_ **S** ). Algorithm C.1 states efficient pseudo-code to construct the **O** -set and detect whether a valid adjustment set exists. Since none of the conditions of Def. 3 for adding collider nodes depends on previously added nodes, the algorithm is orderindependent. The statement occurring in lines 11 and 21 (“No valid adjustment set exists.”) is proven in Thm. 1. If the graph is a DAG, then lines 4-22 can be omitted. Numerical experiments in Section 3 will show that further interesting adjustment sets are the _minimized_ **O** -set **O** min, where **O** is minimized such that no subset can be removed without making **O** min invalid, and the _collider-minimized_ **O** -set **O** Cmin where only **CPC** _\_ **P** _⊆_ **O** is minimized such that no collider-subset can be removed without making **O** Cmin invalid. Both adjustment sets can be constructed with Alg. C.2 similar to the efficient algorithms in van der Zander et al. [2019]. Also the minimized sets are order-independent since the nodes are removed only after the for-loops. Based on the idea in **O** Cmin, in the numerical experiments we also consider AdjustXmin, where only Adjust _\ pa_ ( _Y_ **M** ) is minimized and _pa_ ( _Y_ **M** ) is always included. Finally, we also evaluate Adjustmin where Adjust is fully minimized.

Before discussing the optimality of the **O** -set, we need to assure that it is a valid adjustment set. Similar to the proof given in Perkovi´c et al. [2018] for the validity of the **vancs** -set (for the case without **S** ), we can state that the **O** -set is valid if and only if a valid adjustment set exists.

**Theorem 1** (Validity of O-set) **.** _Given Assumptions 1 but_ without _a priori assuming that a valid adjustment set exists (apart from the requirement_ **S** _∩_ **forb** = _∅). If and only if a valid backdoor adjustment set exists, then_ **O** _is a valid adjustment set._

## **2.4 Graphical optimality**

We now move to the question of optimality. It is known that there are graphs where no graphical criterion exists to determine optimality. Examples, discussed later, are the graphs in Figs. 2E,F.

Before stating necessary and sufficient conditions for graphical optimality, I mention that next to the **O** -set defined above and the Adjust set **vancs** [Perkovi´c et al., 2018], I am not aware of any other systematically constructed set that will yield a valid adjustment set for the case with hidden variables. van der Zander et al. [2019] provide algorithms to list all valid adjustment sets, but the question is which of these a user should choose. As mentioned above, Lemma 1 can be used to cross-compare all pairs of sets, but this is not really feasible. Hence, for automated causal effect estimation, rather than the question of whether graphical optimality holds, it is crucial to have a set with better properties than other systematically constructable sets. In the following the **O** -set is illustrated and conditions for graphical optimality are explored. SSR20 provide a sufficient condition for optimality, which states that either all nodes are observed (no bidirected edges exist) or for all observed nodes **V** _⊂_ **vancs** . This is a very strict assumption and not fulfilled for any of the examples (except for Example G) discussed in the following.

**Example B.** Figure 2B depicts a larger example to illustrate the **O** -set **O** = **PCPC** with **P** = _Z_ 1 _Z_ 2 _Z_ 3 _Z_ 4 (blue boxes) and **CPC** _\_ **P** = _Z_ 5 _Z_ 6 _Z_ 7 _Z_ 8 (green boxes). We also have a conditioned variable **S** . Among **P** , only _Z_ 1 _Z_ 2 are needed to block non-causal paths to _X_ , _Z_ 3 _Z_ 4 are only there to constrain information in _Y_ . Here the same holds for the whole set **CPC** _\_ **P** which was constructed from the paths _Y ↔Z_ 5 _↔Z_ 6 _←Z_ 7 and _Y ↔Z_ 5 _↔Z_ 8 which does not include _Z_ 9 since it is a descendant of _Y_ **M** . Including an independent variable like _Z_ 12 in **O** would not decrease the adjustment information _J_ **O** , but then **O** would not be of minimum cardinality anymore (proven in Cor. B.1). Here, again, the condition of SSR20 does not hold (e.g., _Z_ 5 is not an ancestor of _XY_ **S** ). **O** is optimal here which can be seen as follows: For term (iii) in the inequalities (7) to even be non-zero, we would need a valid **Z** such that **Z** _\_ **O** has a path to _Y_ given **OS** _X_ . But these are all blocked. Note that while _Z_ 10 or _Z_ 9 _∈_ **Z** would open a path to _Y_ , both of these are descendants of **M** or _Y_ and, hence, cannot be in a valid **Z** . For term (iv) to even be non-zero **O** _\_ **Z** would need to have a path to _X_ given **ZS** . But since any valid **Z** has to contain _Z_ 1 and _Z_ 2 (or _Z_ 11), the only nodes in **O** with a path to _X_ are parents of _Y_ **M** and paths from these parents to _X_ all need to be blocked for a valid **Z** . Hence, **O** is optimal here.

## **4 Discussion and Conclusions**

The proposed adjustment information formalizes the common intuition to choose adjustment sets that maximally constrain the effect variable and minimally constrain the cause variable. The main **theoretical contributions** are a necessary and sufficient graphical criterion for the existence of an optimal adjustment set in the hidden variables case and a definition and algorithm to construct it. To emphasize, graphical optimality implies that the **O** -set is optimal for _any distribution_ consistent with the graph. Note that in cases where graphical optimality does not hold, there will still be distributions for which the **O** -set has maximal adjustment information.

Further, the optimal set is valid if and only if a valid adjustment set exists and has smaller (or equal) asymptotic variance compared to the Adjust-set proposed in Perkovi´c et al. [2018] for any graph, whether graphical optimality holds or not. This makes the **O** -set a natural choice in automated causal inference analyses. **Practical contributions** comprise Python code to construct adjustment sets and check optimality, as well as extensive numerical experiments that demonstrate that the theoretical results also hold for relatively small sample sizes.

The theoretical **optimality results are limited** to estimators for which the asymptotic variance becomes minimal for adjustment sets with maximal adjustment information (relation (8)). This is fulfilled for least-squares estimators, where even the direct relation (9) holds, but it is unclear whether this also holds for more general classes. The numerical results show that the **O** -set or minimized variants thereof often yield smaller variance also in non-optimal settings and beyond that estimator class. I speculate that further theoretical properties of maximizing adjustment information can be shown because relation (9) for _f_ ( _·_ ) = ~~_√_~~ 1 _n[e][H][Y][ |][X]_ **[ZS]** _[−][H][X][|]_ **[ZS]**[seems related to the lower bound of the] estimation variance counterpart to Fano’s inequality (Theorem 8.6.6 in Cover and Thomas [2006]). For estimators sensitive to high-dimensionality one may consider data-driven criteria or penalties to step-wisely minimize the **O** -set. However, estimating, for example, the adjustment information from a potentially small sample size carries considerable errors itself. Another current limitation is that relation (9) only holds for univariate singleton cause variables _X_ . The information-theoretical results, however, also hold for multivariate **X** and preliminary results indicate that, while relation (9) does not hold for multivariate **X** , the less restrictive relation (8) still seems to hold.

The proposed information-theoretic approach can guide **further research** , for example, to theoretically study relations (8),(9) for other estimators and to address other types of graphs as emerge from the output of causal discovery algorithms and the setting where the graph is unknown [Witte et al., 2020, Maathuis et al., 2009, 2010]. At present, the approach only applies to ADMGs and _Maximal Ancestral Graphs_ (MAG) [Richardson and Spirtes, 2002] without selection variables. Last, it remains an open problem to identify optimal adjustment estimands for the hidden variables case based on other criteria such as the front-door formula and Pearl’s general do-calculus [Pearl, 2009].

The results may carry considerable **practical impact** since, surprisingly, among the randomly created configurations more than 90% fulfill the optimality conditions indicating that also in many realworld scenarios graphical optimality may hold. zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper provides a significant advancement in causal inference by characterizing the necessary and sufficient graphical conditions for optimal adjustment sets in ADMGs, addressing a major open problem. The information-theoretic framing (maximizing 'adjustment information') is intuitive and elegant, and the results relating the O-set to the previously standard Adjust-set are robust. However, there are minor technical ambiguities regarding the definition of valid collider paths and the justification for the specific estimator class (Relation 9) beyond the linear Gaussian case.

comments.title: Ambiguity in definition of valid collider paths
comments.quote: both of the following conditions are fulfilled: (1) $C \notin \textbf{forb} \cup \textbf{S}$ , and (2) (a) $C \in \textbf{vancs}$ OR (b) $C \perp\!\!\!\perp X | \textbf{vancs}$
comments.explanation: Initially, I was concerned that condition (2a) and (2b) might be mutually exclusive or redundant. However, context suggests the author uses 'vancs' as a base set to test independence. The ambiguity arises because the definition aims to include nodes that help block paths or reduce variance without re-introducing bias. If a node $C$ is in 'vancs', it is already part of the Adjust-set; if it is not, but is independent of $X$ given 'vancs', it can still be added to the O-set. The phrase 'the collider path stops before C' in the prose helps clarify, but the formal logic could be interpreted by a reader as requiring re-evaluation of 'vancs' itself. Nevertheless, the algorithm C.1 clarifies the fixed nature of 'vancs' during the search.

comments.title: Insufficient justification for general estimator class relation
comments.quote: The results translate to minimal asymptotic estimation variance for a class of estimators whose asymptotic variance follows a certain information-theoretic relation that, at present, I could only verify theoretically for the linear case.
comments.explanation: The author builds the entire optimization framework on Relation 9 (the exponential of the entropy difference). While it is shown to hold for OLS in Gaussian models, and the paper acknowledges this limitation, the claim that the results 'translate to minimal asymptotic estimation variance' for a broader class is a non-trivial leap. In non-parametric settings, the asymptotic variance of a plug-in estimator often depends on the conditional variance of the outcome and the density of the treatment (e.g., the propensity score), but not necessarily in the exact subtraction-of-entropies form shown in Eq (9). Without a formal derivation for at least one non-linear estimator (like a specific kernel or series estimator), the 'characterization' of the class remains somewhat speculative.

comments.title: Potential conflict in N-node definition
comments.quote: Denote the set of N-nodes by $\textbf{N} = sp(Y\textbf{MC}) \setminus (\textbf{forb} \cup \textbf{O} \cup \textbf{S})$.
comments.explanation: I checked whether an N-node could simultaneously be required for validity and excluded by this definition. Since $\textbf{O}$ is defined to include all 'parents of $Y\textbf{M}$' and 'valid collider path nodes', excluding $\textbf{O}$ from $\textbf{N}$ makes sense to isolate 'nuisance' nodes. However, Theorem 3 condition (I) relies on constructing an O-set for a modified condition set including an N-node ($\textbf{O}_{\pi iN}$). There is a slight circularity: the optimality of $\textbf{O}$ depends on a property of an N-node, which is defined as being outside $\textbf{O}$. If the graph is modified, the sets $\textbf{P}$ and $\textbf{C}$ would change, potentially moving the node from $\textbf{N}$ to $\textbf{O}$. The logic holds if the reader assumes a 'base' graph, but it requires careful parsing of the 'O-set for the causal effect ... given $\textbf{S}'$' phrasing.

comments.title: Numerical inconsistency regarding 'Adjust-set' inclusion
comments.quote: vancs = $Z_1 Z_2 S$ (in Fig 1A interpretation) ... vancs = $Z_1 Z_4 Z_5$ (in Example C)
comments.explanation: In Section 1.1, the paper defines the Adjust-set (vancs) using equation (2) as $an(XY\textbf{S}) \setminus \textbf{forb}$. In Figure 1A, the text says $\textbf{vancs} = Z_1 Z_2 S$. However, $S$ is already a conditioning variable. In causal literature, the 'adjustment set' usually refers to the variables $Z$ chosen in addition to $S$. If $\textbf{Z}$ is the adjustment set, then $p(Y|do(X)) = \sum p(Y|x, z, s)p(z, s)$. The paper occasionally conflates the 'adjustment set Z' with the 'total set of conditioned variables ZS'. While resolved by looking at the definition of $J_Z$ in Eq (5), it could mislead a reader into double-counting $S$ as part of the variance-reduction set $Z$.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a significant theoretical advance in causal graphical models with hidden variables by providing a necessary and sufficient graphical criterion for the existence of an optimal adjustment set, defined via a novel 'adjustment information' criterion. The work carefully builds on prior literature and addresses a key open problem. The proposed O-set is well-motivated, and the numerical experiments are extensive and informative. However, there are several technical issues in the derivation and notation that affect the clarity and correctness of the main results. In particular, the adjustment information definition appears to have a sign error, and there are inconsistencies in its relationship to asymptotic variance. While the algorithmic and experimental components are strong, these foundational issues need resolution to ensure the validity of the key claims.

comments.title: Sign error in definition of adjustment information
comments.quote: J_\mathbf{Z} = I_{\mathbf{Z};Y|XS} - I_{X;\mathbf{Z}|S}
comments.explanation: The adjustment information is defined as $J_\mathbf{Z} = I_{\mathbf{Z};Y|XS} - I_{X;\mathbf{Z}|S}$. At first glance, this seems intuitive: maximize information between adjustment set and outcome (beneficial), while minimizing information between treatment and adjustment set (avoid overcontrol). However, in Section 2.2, the paper claims that minimizing the 'adjustment entropy' $H(Y|X\mathbf{Z}S) - H(X|\mathbf{Z}S)$ is equivalent to maximizing $J_\mathbf{Z}$. But using the chain rule for mutual information, $I_{\mathbf{Z};Y|XS} = H(Y|XS) - H(Y|X\mathbf{Z}S)$ and $I_{X;\mathbf{Z}|S} = H(X|S) - H(X|\mathbf{Z}S)$. Substituting, $J_\mathbf{Z} = [H(Y|XS) - H(Y|X\mathbf{Z}S)] - [H(X|S) - H(X|\mathbf{Z}S)] = [H(X|\mathbf{Z}S) - H(Y|X\mathbf{Z}S)] + \text{const}$. Thus, maximizing $J_\mathbf{Z}$ corresponds to maximizing $H(X|\mathbf{Z}S) - H(Y|X\mathbf{Z}S)$, not minimizing it. But equation (9) claims asymptotic variance is minimized when this entropy difference is minimized. Therefore, unless the function $f$ in (9) is strictly decreasing (which contradicts the stated 'strictly monotonously increasing'), the sign is flipped. The authors likely intended $J_\mathbf{Z} = I_{X;\mathbf{Z}|S} - I_{\mathbf{Z};Y|XS}$ or to minimize $J_\mathbf{Z}$, but as written, it contradicts the variance relation. This error propagates through the paper, affecting all claims linking $J_\mathbf{Z}$ to estimator variance.

comments.title: Inconsistency in optimal set characterization based on entropy
comments.quote: Minimizing the adjustment entropy is by Eq. (6) equivalent to maximizing the adjustment information.
comments.explanation: Equation (6) states $H(Y|X\mathbf{Z}S) - H(X|\mathbf{Z}S) = -J_\mathbf{Z}$, since $J_\mathbf{Z} = I_{\mathbf{Z};Y|XS} - I_{X;\mathbf{Z}|S} = [H(Y|XS) - H(Y|X\mathbf{Z}S)] - [H(X|S) - H(X|\mathbf{Z}S)]$. So $H(Y|X\mathbf{Z}S) - H(X|\mathbf{Z}S) = -J_\mathbf{Z} + \text{const}$. The text claims minimizing this entropy difference is equivalent to maximizing $J_\mathbf{Z}$, but actually, minimizing the entropy difference is equivalent to *minimizing* $J_\mathbf{Z}$ (or maximizing $-J_\mathbf{Z}$). This directly contradicts the stated equivalence. Given that the goal is to minimize asymptotic variance via minimizing the entropy difference (as per equation 9), the correct quantity to maximize would be $-J_\mathbf{Z}$, not $J_\mathbf{Z}$. This is not resolved by context—it is a fundamental algebraic error.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Lemma 1's inequalities affecting sufficiency claim
comments.quote: if and only if there is a Z ∈ Z such that either there is no other Z′ ≠ Z ∈ Z or for all other Z′ ≠ Z ∈ Z and all distributions P consistent with G it holds that I_{Z∖Z′; Y|XSZ′} ≥ I_{X; Z′∖Z|SZ} and I_{Z′∖Z; Y|XSZ} ≤ I_{X; Z∖Z′|SZ′}
comments.explanation: Lemma 1 presents two inequalities (7) as a necessary and sufficient condition for graphical optimality. However, the indices in the conditional sets are inconsistent: the first inequality conditions on $Z'$ in the RHS ($I_{X; Z'\setminus Z | S Z'}$) while the second uses $S$ only ($I_{X; Z\setminus Z' | S Z'}$). Given the symmetry of the comparison, one would expect $SZ'$ in both or a consistent pattern. Later, the author refers to 'terms (iii) and (iv)' suggesting a four-term expansion possibly from mutual information chain rules. But without a derivation, it's unclear whether the conditioning sets are correctly specified. The asymmetry could indicate a typo. If the second term should condition on $SZ$, that would make the expression more plausible. As written, the condition is ambiguous and could lead a reader to misapply it. However, given that this is a core lemma and later examples interpret it graphically, it's plausible that the graphical interpretation (blocking paths) resolves the intent, making this more of an ambiguity than a fatal flaw.

comments.title: O-set validity theorem lacks proof and conflicts with construction
comments.quote: If and only if a valid backdoor adjustment set exists, then O is a valid adjustment set.
comments.explanation: Theorem 1 asserts that the O-set is valid iff any valid adjustment set exists, which is a strong and useful claim. However, no proof is provided in the main text or referenced as being in the supplement. The construction of the O-set involves valid collider paths dependent on **vancs**, but it's not immediately obvious why adding spouses and their parents preserves validity or why this set doesn't include forbidden nodes. For example, in Fig. 2B, adding $Z_7$ (parent of $Z_6$, a collider on a path to $Y$) could potentially open paths if $Z_7$ has descendants in **M** or $Y$. The algorithm checks **forb**, but the formal justification is absent. While the claim may be correct, the lack of proof or reference to supplement in this critical result makes it underspecified. Given that Perković et al. established validity for **vancs**, a similar argument is expected here.

comments.title: Overstatement of estimator class applicability
comments.quote: The results translate to minimal asymptotic estimation variance for a class of estimators whose asymptotic variance follows a certain information-theoretic relation.
comments.explanation: The paper repeatedly claims that the results 'translate to' minimal asymptotic variance for a broad class, but then admits (correctly) that only the linear Gaussian case is verified. Given that the adjustment information definition appears to have a sign error (as noted above), the relation to variance is even more questionable. The numerical experiments test other estimators, but do not validate the theoretical relation (9) or (8). Claiming translation to minimal variance without proof for non-linear, non-parametric cases is a questionable claim. While empirical results suggest benefits, the theoretical 'translation' is currently only solid for a narrow class. This is softened by the numerical section, but the text overstates the theoretical generality.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: This paper makes significant theoretical contributions to causal inference by providing necessary and sufficient conditions for optimal adjustment sets in graphical models with hidden variables. comments.title: Ambiguous notation in adjustment information definition
comments.quote: The quantity to maximize can be stated as follows. Definition 1 (Adjustment information). Consider a causal effect of X on Y for an adjustment set Z given a condition set S. The (conditional) adjustment (set) information, abbreviated J_Z, is defined as J_Z = I_Z;Y|XS - IX;Z|S
comments.explanation: The notation I_Z;Y|XS in Definition 1 is ambiguous. Standard notation would be I(Z;Y|XS), but the subscript notation I_Z;Y is inconsistent with this. While the meaning becomes clearer from Eq. (6) which expresses J_Z in terms of conditional entropies, the initial definition could confuse readers familiar with standard mutual information notation. The author should clarify whether I_Z;Y|XS means I(Z;Y|XS) or use more standard notation throughout.

comments.title: Unclear connection between collider path definition and algorithm conclusion
comments.quote: Definition 3 (Valid collider paths). Given a graph G, a collider path of W for k ≥ 1 is defined by a sequence of edges W ↔C_1 ↔· · · ↔Ck... We call a collider path node set πW[i] for W∈Y_M valid wrt. (X, Y, S) if for each path node C ∈ πW[i] both of the following conditions are fulfilled: (2b) C ⊥⊥ X | vancs... Algorithm C.1 states efficient pseudo-code to construct the O-set and detect whether a valid adjustment set exists. Since none of the conditions of Def. 3 for adding collider nodes depends on previously added nodes, the algorithm is order-independent. The statement occurring in lines 11 and 21 ('No valid adjustment set exists.') is proven in Thm. 1.
comments.explanation: The connection between Definition 3's condition (2b) and the algorithm's conclusion that no valid adjustment set exists is not clearly explained in the main text. If a collider node C fails condition (2b) (i.e., is not independent of X given vancs), why does this imply no valid adjustment set exists? This crucial logical leap is stated but not justified, making it difficult for readers to follow the reasoning. While the proof is in Thm. 1, the main text should provide at least some intuition for this important step.

comments.title: Potentially overstated claim about real-world applicability
comments.quote: Surprisingly, among the randomly created setups more than 90% fulfill the optimality conditions indicating that also in many real-world scenarios graphical optimality may hold.
comments.explanation: This claim extrapolates from randomly generated graphs to real-world scenarios without sufficient justification. Random graphs may have structural properties that differ systematically from real-world causal graphs (e.g., degree distributions, clustering coefficients). The author should be more cautious about this extrapolation or provide evidence that the random graph generation process produces graphs representative of real-world causal structures.

comments.title: Insufficient detail on experimental setup
comments.quote: The experiments are based on a generalized additive model and described in detail in Section D. Among these 12 _,_ 000 randomly created configurations 93% fulfill the optimality conditions in Thm. 3.
comments.explanation: The main text provides insufficient detail about the experimental setup for reproducibility. While Section D in the supplement may have details, the main text should at least specify: (1) how the graphs are generated (what distribution over graph structures?), (2) the specific form of the generalized additive model, and (3) how the 'randomly created configurations' are sampled. Without these details, readers cannot properly evaluate the experimental results.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation in forbidden set definition
comments.quote: To this end define forb = des(YM) \ {Y}... Z4 ∈ forb cannot be included in any set because it is a descendant of Y M
comments.explanation: The notation 'YM' is ambiguous. It could mean Y∪M, or it could be a typo for 'Y or M'. Looking at Figure 1A, Z4 appears to be a descendant of M, not Y. The text should clarify whether 'YM' means the union of Y and M's descendants, or something else. This ambiguity could confuse readers about what exactly constitutes the forbidden set.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: The paper makes a significant contribution toward solving the problem of optimal adjustment in causal graphical models with hidden variables. By using an information-theoretic framework to define 'adjustment information' and deriving a necessary and sufficient graphical criterion for optimality, the author moves past the limitations of previous heuristic or sufficient-only approaches. The theoretical development is logically structured, although the paper contains a minor discrepancy in the definition of a specific set in the text compared to the provided equations, and some mathematical notation involving indices and sets requires careful reader navigation. Overall, the work is highly relevant and the provided empirical results support the practical utility of the proposed methodology.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of the N-node set
comments.quote: Denote the set of N-nodes by N = sp(Y_M) \ (forb ∪ S).
comments.explanation: In Theorem 3, the set of N-nodes is defined as $N = sp(Y_M) \ (forb \cup S)$. However, the notation 'forbOS' appears in the subscript which likely refers to the union of sets mentioned in the setup. Strictly speaking, based on the provided text, 'forbOS' is an undefined symbol that seems to be an OCR error or a typographical collision for $(forb \cup O \cup S)$ or similar. While context implies it stands for the excluded variables, the definition is technically ambiguous as written.

comments.title: Ambiguity in indices for collider paths
comments.quote: For all N ∈ N and all its collider paths i to W ∈ Y_M that are inside C it holds that O_π_iN
comments.explanation: The notation $O_\pi_iN$ is introduced without a formal definition of how the adjustment set $O$ is modified by the path $\pi_i$ specific to $N$. While the intuition suggests this is a subset of the $O$-set conditioned on $N$ and the path node set, the dependency between the subscript index $i$ and the set construction $O(\cdot)$ is underspecified in the formal definition section. A reader cannot independently reproduce the exact set $O_{\pi_iN}$ from the provided definitions.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper presents a novel information-theoretic framework for identifying optimal adjustment sets in causal graphical models with hidden variables, offering a necessary and sufficient graphical criterion (Theorem 3) and a constructive algorithm. The theoretical development is rigorous, logically structured, and builds coherently on prior work (HPM19, SSR20). However, the manuscript suffers from notable notational inconsistencies, particularly regarding the union of the effect and mediator variables, and critical formulas (forbidden set, valid ancestors, Gaussian entropy) are either missing or severely garbled by OCR, hindering reproducibility and verification. Additionally, the authors make a speculative qualitative leap in extrapolating synthetic optimality rates to real-world applicability without empirical validation on observed datasets. These issues are primarily localizable and do not undermine the core theoretical contributions, but they require careful revision before publication.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for effect and mediator union
comments.quote: The mediator nodes on causal paths from X to Y are denoted **M** = **M**(X, Y) and exclude X and Y. ... define the set_ **O** ( _X, Y,_ **S** ) = **P** _∪_ **C** _∪_ **PC** _where_ ... pa(YM ∪ C) ... W ∈ Y M _[ indexed by i]
comments.explanation: Throughout the paper, the union of the effect variable Y and the set of mediators M is referred to with varying, ambiguous notation: 'YM', 'Y M', and 'YMC'. Given that M explicitly excludes Y, the intended meaning is almost certainly the set union Y ∪ M (or a subscripted variant like Y_M). However, the lack of a consistent union symbol, spacing, or clear subscript convention creates unnecessary ambiguity. A careful reader might misinterpret 'YM' as a single latent variable or a product. Standardizing this to, for example, Y_M = Y ∪ M or consistently using Y ∪ M would significantly improve readability and prevent misinterpretation of downstream definitions like pa(YM) and the O-set construction.

comments.title: Missing/garbled foundational formulas hinder verification
comments.quote: define (henceforth just denoted as **forb** ). ... In our setting including conditioning variables **S** we call this set the _valid ancestors_ defined as ... refer to this set as **vancs** or Adjust-set. ... H_ ( _Y |X **ZS** ) = 2[1][+][1] 2[ln(2] _[πσ] Y_[2] _|X **ZS**[)][
comments.explanation: The OCR extraction appears to have dropped the explicit mathematical formulas for the forbidden set (**forb**), the valid ancestors set (**vancs**), and the Gaussian conditional entropy expression. While the surrounding text references these equations (e.g., Eq. 1, Eq. 2, and the entropy expansion in Sec. 2.2), their absence makes it impossible to verify the exact sets being constructed or the constants in the entropy variance relation. Even assuming standard definitions (e.g., **forb** = desc(M ∪ Y) ∪ desc(S) \ S, and entropy = 1/2 ln(2πeσ²)), the missing text obscures whether the authors use natural or base-2 logarithms, and whether the factor e is correctly included. These are critical for reproducing Algorithm C.1 and verifying Eq. (9). I recommend providing the explicit formulas in the main text or a supplementary table to ensure reproducibility.

comments.title: Overclaim regarding real-world applicability based on synthetic data
comments.quote: Surprisingly, among the randomly created setups more than 90% fulfill the optimality conditions indicating that also in many real-world scenarios graphical optimality may hold.
comments.explanation: The authors repeatedly state that the >90% optimality rate observed in their random synthetic graph experiments suggests graphical optimality may commonly hold in real-world scenarios. While the high synthetic success rate is an interesting empirical observation, it does not logically imply real-world prevalence. Real-world causal graphs often contain specific structural complexities, selection biases, or unmeasured confounding patterns that differ from uniform random graph generation. Without validating the optimality conditions on at least a few established real-world benchmark graphs or observational studies, this extrapolation remains speculative. I recommend toning down the phrasing to reflect that the criterion is *frequently satisfied in simulated regimes* and merits investigation on empirical graphs, rather than claiming real-world applicability.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Theorem 3 Condition I phrasing
comments.quote: (I) For all N ∈ N and all its collider paths i to W ∈ Y M ... it holds that O_{π_i^N} does not block all non-causal paths from X to Y, i.e., O_{π_i^N} is non-valid,
comments.explanation: Condition (I) of Theorem 3 uses the term 'non-valid' to mean that the constructed O-set (with extended conditioning) fails to block all non-causal paths. While the parenthetical clarification helps, the phrasing is dense and could be misread. Specifically, 'O_{π_i^N}' is defined as the O-set computed for the causal effect given an expanded condition set S' = S ∪ {N} ∪ π_i^N. A reader might initially wonder whether 'non-valid' refers to the set's internal consistency, its cardinality, or its d-separation properties. The author correctly clarifies it in Example F, but the condition would benefit from explicitly stating 'fails to satisfy the generalized adjustment criterion' or 'does not block all backdoor paths'. This is a framing/ambiguity issue that could mislead readers skimming the theorem.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: This paper makes a substantial contribution to the problem of selecting optimal adjustment sets in causal graphical models with hidden variables, providing a novel information-theoretic characterization and a necessary and sufficient graphical condition for optimality. The theoretical claims are ambitious and the numerical experiments are extensive. However, the presentation suffers from a few gaps that hinder full comprehension, most notably the missing explicit statement of the key inequality (7) in Lemma 1. Additionally, some definitions (e.g., N-nodes, the recursive condition in Theorem 3) are ambiguous or insufficiently justified. These issues are moderate and localized; they do not invalidate the core results but require clarification for rigorous reproducibility.

comments.title: Missing explicit statement of inequality (7) in Lemma 1
comments.quote: if and only if there is a Z ∈ Z such that either there is no other Z' ≠ Z ∈ Z or for all other Z' ≠ Z ∈ Z and all distributions P consistent with G it holds that ... (terms (iii) and (iv) in the inequalities (7))
comments.explanation: The paper refers to 'inequalities (7)' but does not display them. The reader is left to guess what terms (iii) and (iv) are, which are central to the lemma. While the surrounding text mentions conditional independence statements, the exact form of the inequalities is essential for understanding the necessary and sufficient comparison criterion. This omission undermines the clarity of a key theoretical building block.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of N-nodes
comments.quote: Denote the set of N-nodes by N = sp(YMC) \ (forbOS).
comments.explanation: The notation sp(YMC) is not defined for a set of variables; earlier the paper defines sp(X) for a single variable. It likely means the set of spouses of any node in Y∪M∪C, but this should be stated explicitly. Similarly, 'forbOS' could be interpreted as the union of forb, O, and S, but the parentheses are ambiguous. Since N-nodes are crucial for Theorem 3, this lack of precision may confuse readers.

comments.title: Condition (I) uses recursive O-set definition without guarantee of existence or uniqueness
comments.quote: denote by O_{π_i^N} = O(X,Y,S' = S N π_i^N) the O-set for the causal effect of X on Y given S' = S ∪ {N} ∪ π_i^N. If and only if ... both of the following conditions are fulfilled ... (I) For all N ∈ N and all its collider paths i to W ∈ YM that are inside C it holds that O_{π_i^N} does not block all non-causal paths from X to Y, i.e., O_{π_i^N} is non-valid.
comments.explanation: Condition (I) requires evaluating the O-set for a modified conditioning set S'. The paper does not discuss whether this O-set is always well-defined (e.g., if a valid adjustment set exists for that new problem). Theorem 1 states that the O-set is valid iff a valid set exists, so if no valid set exists for (X,Y,S'), the O-set is non-valid by definition. However, it is not clear whether the definition of the O-set (Algorithm C.1) can be applied when no valid set exists, or whether it returns a set at all. The recursive nature of the condition, embedded in the main theorem, requires careful justification that is not provided.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents a significant theoretical advancement in determining optimal adjustment sets in causal graphical models with hidden variables using an information-theoretic framework. The contributions are clearly articulated and supported by numerical experiments. However, there are two specific issues that need attention: a definition error in the conditional mutual information that contradicts standard theory, and a notation ambiguity in Theorem 3 that could confuse the application of the optimality conditions.
